stem: A 30-month-old boy is brought to his GP with 36 hours of right ear pain and fever to 38.6 degrees Celsius. He has been miserable but is feeding well and is alert between febrile episodes. He has had one prior episode of unilateral acute otitis media 8 months ago that resolved with observation. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander and is fully immunised.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.6 degrees Celsius, HR 130, RR 26, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Right tympanic membrane bulging and erythematous with reduced mobility on pneumatic otoscopy; no otorrhoea; left tympanic membrane normal; chest clear; throat unremarkable

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Symptomatic analgesia with regular paracetamol and review in 48 hours if not improving
options.rationale: Australian guidelines support watchful waiting in children over 2 years with unilateral non-severe acute otitis media; antibiotics are added if not improving at 48 hours or worsening. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Acute otitis media)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg twice daily for 5 days commenced immediately today
options.rationale: Immediate antibiotics are indicated for bilateral AOM under 2 years, otorrhoea, Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander children, severe illness or systemic toxicity; this child meets none of those criteria. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Acute otitis media)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg twice daily for 5 days commenced now
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is reserved for treatment failure after 48-72 hours of amoxicillin or for recurrent AOM, not for initial therapy of straightforward unilateral disease. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Acute otitis media)

options.text: Oral cefuroxime axetil 15 mg/kg twice daily for 5 days as a cephalosporin alternative
options.rationale: Oral cephalosporins are reserved for non-severe penicillin allergy and are not first-line in AOM; their pneumococcal pharmacokinetics are inferior to amoxicillin. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Acute otitis media)

options.text: Intramuscular ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg as a single dose given the risk of mastoiditis
options.rationale: Single-dose IM ceftriaxone is reserved for children vomiting all oral antibiotics or with non-severe penicillin allergy when oral options are inappropriate; it is not first-line in well children. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute otitis media)

explanation.summary: Watchful waiting with safety-net analgesia is supported in children over 2 years with unilateral non-severe AOM in Australian guidelines. Immediate antibiotics are reserved for children under 2 with bilateral disease, otorrhoea, Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander children, or systemic toxicity. Amoxicillin-clavulanate and cephalosporins have specific second-line indications.
key_points: Children over 2 years with unilateral non-severe AOM can be safely observed for 48 hours
Amoxicillin-clavulanate is reserved for AOM treatment failure or recurrent disease
Single-dose IM ceftriaxone is reserved for those unable to tolerate oral antibiotics
explanation.pearls: The most reliable AOM antibiotic indications in Australia are bilateral disease under 2 years, otorrhoea at any age, Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander identity and systemic toxicity.

stem: An 18-month-old girl presents to her GP with a 2-day history of fever to 38.4 degrees Celsius, oral pain and a rash on her hands and feet. She is still drinking water and dilute juice but has refused solids for 24 hours. She attends family day care 3 days per week. Her vaccinations are up to date and she is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.4 degrees Celsius, HR 130, RR 28, SpO2 99% in room air; wet nappies in last 4 hours
data_table.Examination: Multiple shallow grey ulcers on tongue and buccal mucosa; vesicles on palms and soles and around perioral skin; no neck stiffness; chest clear; abdomen soft

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days targeting herpes simplex replication
options.rationale: Aciclovir has no role in enteroviral hand foot and mouth disease; it is reserved for herpes simplex stomatitis which typically causes ulcers on the anterior tongue and gingiva without acral vesicles. (Source: RCH CPG: Hand, foot and mouth disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Hand, foot and mouth disease

options.text: Symptomatic analgesia with paracetamol, oral fluids, and day care exclusion until blisters dry
options.rationale: Hand foot and mouth disease is self-limiting; management is analgesia and hydration, with day care exclusion until all vesicles have dried as per NCIRS recommendations. (Source: NCIRS: Enteroviruses and hand, foot and mouth disease)
source_refs: NCIRS: Enteroviruses and hand, foot and mouth disease

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg twice daily for 5 days targeting bacterial pharyngitis as cause
options.rationale: Bacterial pharyngitis would not produce vesicles on the hands and feet; antibiotics are not indicated for the enteroviral exanthem. (Source: RCH CPG: Hand, foot and mouth disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Hand, foot and mouth disease

options.text: Topical viscous lignocaine 2% to the oral ulcers four times daily as topical analgesia
options.rationale: Topical lignocaine carries a risk of seizures and methaemoglobinaemia in young children and is not recommended in this age group for HFM. (Source: RCH CPG: Hand, foot and mouth disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Hand, foot and mouth disease

options.text: Oral nystatin 100,000 units four times daily as antifungal mouth therapy for oral ulcers
options.rationale: Oral candida produces white plaques rather than ulcers and acral vesicles; nystatin is not indicated in the enteroviral exanthem. (Source: RCH CPG: Hand, foot and mouth disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Hand, foot and mouth disease

explanation.summary: Hand foot and mouth disease is a self-limiting enteroviral illness with oral ulcers and acral vesicles. Management is analgesia, hydration and day care exclusion until all vesicles have dried. Topical lignocaine should be avoided in young children due to seizure and methaemoglobinaemia risk. Antibiotics, antivirals and antifungals are not indicated.
key_points: Hand foot mouth disease is supportive only with analgesia and hydration
Day care exclusion is required until all vesicles have dried per NCIRS
Topical lignocaine is unsafe in young children for oral ulcer pain
explanation.pearls: Watch for dehydration as the main complication; admit if a child cannot maintain oral intake or develops neurological signs suggesting rare enteroviral encephalitis.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Hand, foot and mouth disease
NCIRS: Enteroviruses and hand, foot and mouth disease

stem: A 4-month-old girl is brought to the emergency department with 2 days of fever and reduced feeding. There are no respiratory symptoms and no rash. She is the first child of well parents, was born at term and has had no prior infections. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. A catheter specimen of urine shows leucocytes and nitrites with bacteria on microscopy.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.4 degrees Celsius, HR 170, RR 40, SpO2 98% in room air, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Alert but irritable; anterior fontanelle soft; chest clear; abdomen soft with no mass; nappy area normal
data_table.Investigations: Catheter urine: leucocytes 4+, nitrites positive, bacteria 3+; bloods: WCC 18, CRP 75, creatinine 30; blood culture pending

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Oral trimethoprim 4 mg/kg twice daily for 5 days at home with GP review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months with febrile UTI require initial parenteral therapy due to the risk of bacteraemia and pyelonephritis; oral therapy alone is inappropriate at this age. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg three times daily for 7 days as outpatient management today
options.rationale: Outpatient oral therapy is not appropriate for febrile UTI under 6 months; admission for IV antibiotics is required while culture and sensitivities return. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Admit for IV gentamicin 7 mg/kg daily and IV benzylpenicillin while culture results return
options.rationale: Febrile UTI in infants under 6 months requires admission for empirical IV gentamicin plus benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin per Australian paediatric guidance; transition to oral therapy follows clinical improvement and sensitivities. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Urinary tract infection in children)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: Urinary tract infection in children

options.text: Discharge with a single intramuscular ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg and outpatient follow-up tomorrow
options.rationale: Single-dose IM ceftriaxone with outpatient follow-up is not the standard Australian approach in young infants and risks under-treatment of bacteraemia. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Discharge with oral amoxicillin and arrange an outpatient renal tract ultrasound next week
options.rationale: Discharge on oral amoxicillin alone is unsafe in a young infant with febrile UTI; renal tract imaging is appropriate after acute treatment but is not the initial step. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: Febrile UTI in infants under 6 months is managed with admission for empirical IV gentamicin and a beta-lactam in Australia, given the risk of bacteraemia and pyelonephritis. Oral therapy alone, single-dose IM ceftriaxone, and outpatient discharge are inappropriate at this age. Renal tract imaging is arranged after acute treatment.
key_points: Infants under 6 months with febrile UTI need admission for IV antibiotics
Empirical regimen is IV gentamicin plus benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin
Outpatient oral therapy is inappropriate due to bacteraemia risk at this age
explanation.pearls: After first febrile UTI in any child under 6 months, arrange renal tract ultrasound; consider DMSA at 4-6 months and MCUG only if there is high recurrence or imaging abnormality.

sources.label: eTG Antibiotic: Urinary tract infection in children

stem: An 18-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department after a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes at home in the setting of fever to 39.2 degrees Celsius. He has had 24 hours of cough and rhinorrhoea. He has no prior seizures and his development is normal. He is now alert though sleepy. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.8 degrees Celsius, HR 130, RR 28, SpO2 99% in room air, capillary glucose 5.2
data_table.Examination: GCS 15, no focal neurology, anterior fontanelle closed and not bulging, no neck stiffness, no petechial rash, ENT normal, chest clear

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Admit for lumbar puncture given age under 24 months and a seizure in the setting of fever
options.rationale: LP is no longer recommended on age alone in a well, alert, fully vaccinated child with a simple febrile seizure; it is reserved for signs of meningitis or atypical features. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

options.text: Commence daily prophylactic sodium valproate 20 mg/kg/day to prevent further febrile seizures
options.rationale: Daily anti-seizure medication is not recommended for simple febrile seizures; the benign natural history and the side effects of valproate outweigh any small reduction in recurrence. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

options.text: Arrange an outpatient EEG within 1 week to characterise the seizure type before discharge
options.rationale: EEG is not routinely indicated for a simple febrile seizure; it does not predict recurrence or epilepsy and is reserved for atypical or complex features. (Source: NSW Health: Febrile convulsions in children)
source_refs: NSW Health: Febrile convulsions in children

options.text: Reassure the family, advise antipyretics for comfort, and provide written first-aid seizure advice
options.rationale: Simple febrile seizures are benign with a low risk of epilepsy; management is reassurance, antipyretics for comfort (which do not prevent recurrence) and written seizure first-aid advice. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

options.text: Prescribe buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg for parents to use if any future seizure exceeds 5 minutes
options.rationale: Rescue benzodiazepines are reserved for children with prolonged or recurrent complex febrile seizures, not after a single simple febrile seizure of 2 minutes. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

explanation.summary: Simple febrile seizures are brief generalised tonic-clonic events in well children aged 6 months to 5 years and carry a benign prognosis. Management is reassurance, antipyretics for comfort and written first-aid advice. Lumbar puncture is reserved for suspected meningitis. Daily anti-seizure medication and routine EEG are not indicated.
key_points: Simple febrile seizure is brief, generalised, single in 24 hours, in a well child
Antipyretics improve comfort but do not prevent febrile seizure recurrence
LP, EEG and daily anticonvulsants are not routinely indicated
explanation.pearls: Buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg is reserved for children with previous febrile status epilepticus or known epilepsy, not after a first simple febrile seizure.

sources.label: NSW Health: Febrile convulsions in children

stem: A 5-year-old boy (20 kg) with known peanut allergy collapsed at a birthday party 15 minutes ago after eating a satay skewer. The ambulance crew gave intramuscular adrenaline 0.15 mg in the lateral thigh en route and again 5 minutes later on arrival in the emergency department. IV access has been established. He remains audibly stridorous with widespread urticaria.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 38, SpO2 90% on 8 L oxygen via mask, BP 65/35, HR 165, GCS 14
data_table.Examination: Audible inspiratory stridor and expiratory wheeze, generalised urticaria, perioral angioedema, cool peripheries, capillary refill 4 seconds
data_table.Medications: Two IM adrenaline 0.15 mg doses given 5 minutes apart; IV crystalloid 20 mL/kg bolus commenced

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Third intramuscular adrenaline 0.15 mg dose with a second IV crystalloid bolus of 20 mL/kg
options.rationale: ASCIA recommends that anaphylaxis failing to improve after 2 IM adrenaline doses is escalated to a controlled IV adrenaline infusion rather than further IM doses, due to absorption variability in shock. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg and oral cetirizine 5 mg as adjunct anti-inflammatory therapy
options.rationale: Corticosteroids and antihistamines are second-line adjuncts and do not reverse airway compromise or shock; they are not the immediate priority in refractory anaphylaxis. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg in 5 mL of saline for ongoing upper airway swelling and stridor
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is an adjunct for upper airway oedema but does not address systemic hypotension; an IV adrenaline infusion is preferred when refractory shock coexists. (Source: RCH CPG: Anaphylaxis)

options.text: IM adrenaline 0.5 mg using the adult dose to overcome refractory severe symptoms
options.rationale: Adult-dose IM adrenaline is not weight-appropriate in a 20 kg child; further IM dosing is not the recommended escalation when 2 doses have failed. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Commence IV adrenaline infusion at 0.1 mcg/kg/min titrated and continue IV fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: When anaphylaxis is refractory after 2 IM adrenaline doses, ASCIA recommends a controlled IV adrenaline infusion at 0.1 mcg/kg/min titrated to response with ongoing fluid resuscitation. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

explanation.summary: Anaphylaxis refractory to 2 doses of IM adrenaline is managed with an IV adrenaline infusion at 0.1 mcg/kg/min titrated to response, in addition to continued fluid resuscitation and airway support. Corticosteroids, antihistamines and nebulised adrenaline are adjuncts rather than primary therapy. Adult dosing and further IM doses are not recommended in refractory paediatric shock.
key_points: IV adrenaline infusion is indicated when anaphylaxis fails to respond to 2 IM doses
Starting rate is 0.1 mcg/kg/min titrated to clinical response in monitored settings
Fluid resuscitation continues alongside vasoactive escalation
explanation.pearls: Always seek airway expertise early in refractory anaphylaxis; angioedema can progress rapidly and a small calibre paediatric airway is at risk during ongoing resuscitation.

sources.label: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines
